skill.name: wp-playground
skill.description: Create browser-based WordPress Playground environments for plugin testing, WooCommerce demos, theme previews, and temporary development without Docker.
skill.summary: Launch disposable WordPress Playground test sites from browser links, CLI commands, and reusable blueprints.
skill.author: CrazySwami

security_audit.summary: Static high-severity weak-cryptography alerts were false positives on ordinary WordPress text fields and descriptions. The skill is legitimate WordPress Playground guidance, but it has medium operational risk because it instructs users to run npx commands, enable Playground networking, mount local directories, and use blueprint steps that can execute PHP or WP-CLI.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Commands and Local Mounts
medium_findings.description: The skill documents npx-based WordPress Playground commands and local directory mounts. This is legitimate tooling, but users should review commands before running them because npx may download packages and mounts expose local project files to the Playground server.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and operational, including npx execution and local directory mounting. The context is legitimate development documentation, so this is a medium operational risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Code-Capable Playground Blueprint Steps
medium_findings.description: The skill documents runPHP, wp-cli, and writeFile blueprint steps, and the base blueprint includes a static runPHP step. These capabilities are expected for WordPress Playground but could be risky if users import untrusted blueprints.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capability is clearly present, and the included PHP only creates sample pages. The risk depends on user-supplied or remote blueprints, so confidence is medium-high rather than very high.

medium_findings.title: Network-Enabled Playground Blueprints
medium_findings.description: The bundled blueprints enable Playground networking and install plugins or themes from WordPress.org. This is normal for WordPress testing, but it creates external network dependency and supply-chain exposure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Networking and external plugin installation are directly documented. The endpoints are expected WordPress resources, so the issue is exposure and dependency risk, not evidence of exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged weak-cryptography locations are WordPress descriptions, schema references, or site option text. I found no hash algorithm, cipher selection, credential handling, or cryptographic operation at these lines.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows ordinary descriptive strings at every reported location. There is no code path that performs cryptography.

low_findings.title: Documented Hidden Directory Path
low_findings.description: The hidden home-directory path points to the expected local Claude skills folder for blueprint templates. It is a filesystem reference, but I found no instruction to read secrets or exfiltrate hidden files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only documents where templates are stored. It does not include commands that read arbitrary hidden files or sensitive user data.

low_findings.title: Email Capability Alert Is a False Positive
low_findings.description: The email-related static alert appears in a comparison table saying Playground has no email support and Docker may support SMTP. No email sending code or credential handling was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed line is comparative documentation, not an implementation or instruction to send email. It explicitly states that Playground email is not available.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Expected Documentation
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the official WordPress Playground site, the Playground blueprint schema, WordPress resources, or a generic example blueprint URL. I found no evidence that these URLs exfiltrate data or contact an unrelated service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms the URLs are documentation examples and known WordPress Playground resources. The remote blueprint example still warrants user review, but it is not evidence of malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: npx Package Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to run @wp-playground/cli through npx. This can execute package code on the user machine and should be reviewed before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and executable. The package is a legitimate WordPress Playground tool, so the risk is controlled but real.

dangerous_patterns.title: Blueprint Code Execution Steps
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents runPHP and wp-cli steps, and a bundled blueprint uses runPHP for sample content. These steps execute inside Playground and should not be accepted from untrusted blueprints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code-capable steps are directly present. The bundled code is benign, but the pattern can become dangerous with untrusted input.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Directory Mounting
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows mounting local plugin or theme directories into the Playground environment. This is useful for development but exposes selected local project files to the running server.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The mount commands are clear and intentional. Risk is limited to directories the user chooses to mount.

content.value_statement: WordPress setup often slows down quick plugin tests and demos. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code create disposable Playground environments with reusable blueprints.
actual_capabilities: Provides quick-start browser and CLI workflows for WordPress Playground.
Includes base and WooCommerce blueprint examples for repeatable test environments.
Shows URL parameters for installing plugins, themes, and specific WordPress versions.
Explains local plugin and theme mounting for development previews.
Demonstrates JavaScript API usage for programmatic Playground control.
Compares Playground with Docker for testing and demo scenarios.
limitations: Playground environments are temporary unless users export or persist data.
Performance is slower than native PHP because WordPress runs through WebAssembly.
Networking and WP-CLI behavior are limited compared with Docker.
The skill provides guidance and templates, not a managed hosting environment.

use_cases.title: Plugin Compatibility Check
use_cases.description: Spin up a temporary WordPress site with selected plugins and versions before installing them elsewhere.

use_cases.title: WooCommerce Demo Build
use_cases.description: Create a disposable store preview with WooCommerce and Storefront for client review or internal testing.

use_cases.title: Theme Preview Session
use_cases.description: Mount a local theme directory and inspect changes in a browser without setting up Docker.

prompt_templates.title: Start a Basic Playground
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-playground skill to show me the simplest browser and CLI options for starting a temporary WordPress Playground site.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Plugin Test Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-playground skill to plan a temporary WordPress Playground test for WooCommerce, Yoast SEO, and a selected WordPress version.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Reusable Blueprint
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-playground skill to design a reusable Playground blueprint for a WordPress development site with common plugins and site options.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Playground and Docker
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the wp-playground skill to compare WordPress Playground and Docker for my plugin workflow, including persistence, networking, WP-CLI, and performance tradeoffs.

output_examples.input: Set up a quick WooCommerce test site.
output_examples.output: A short setup plan with a Playground URL option, a CLI option, recommended WooCommerce blueprint settings, and cleanup notes.

output_examples.input: Preview my local theme without Docker.
output_examples.output: A local mount workflow that identifies the theme directory, starts Playground with the right mount target, and lists browser testing steps.

output_examples.input: Should I use Playground or Docker for this plugin?
output_examples.output: A practical comparison covering setup time, persistence, networking, WP-CLI needs, performance, and the recommended environment.

best_practices: Review every npx command and blueprint before running it locally.
Use WordPress.org plugin and theme sources when building shared demos.
Export snapshots or document setup steps when a test site must be recreated.
anti_patterns: Do not use Playground as a substitute for production-like performance testing.
Do not load untrusted blueprint URLs without reviewing their steps.
Do not mount directories that contain secrets, private keys, or unrelated project data.

faq.question: Does this skill create a permanent WordPress site?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on temporary WordPress Playground environments unless users export snapshots or configure persistence.

faq.question: Can it test WooCommerce?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill includes a WooCommerce blueprint and guidance for store demos and plugin checks.

faq.question: Does it require Docker?
faq.answer: No. Playground runs WordPress through WebAssembly in the browser or through the Playground CLI.

faq.question: Can it mount my local plugin or theme?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill documents CLI mount options for local plugin and theme directories.

faq.question: Is networking enabled?
faq.answer: The included blueprints enable Playground networking for plugin and theme installation from WordPress resources.

faq.question: What should I review before running commands?
faq.answer: Review npx commands, blueprint URLs, runPHP steps, WP-CLI commands, and any local directories selected for mounting.
